title: Property Management Operations Score — Where Is Your Team Losing Hours?
subtitle: A 15-minute diagnostic for property management owners running 10-75 employees. Pinpoint the exact workflows bleeding 15-30+ hours a week so you can scale without scaling payroll.

persona_selector.text: Which best describes your property management firm?

answers.label: Boutique (10-20 employees, under ~500 doors)
Growing (21-35 employees, ~500-1,200 doors)
Established (36-55 employees, ~1,200-2,500 doors)
Scaling (56-75 employees, 2,500+ doors)

questions.text: How quickly does your team respond to inbound tenant messages across email, SMS, and portal?

answers.label: 1 — Often 24+ hours, depending on who is covering
2 — Same-day during business hours, nothing after
3 — Within a few hours during business hours
4 — Within an hour via templated replies plus triage
5 — Instant acknowledgement 24/7 with AI triage and routing

questions.text: How consolidated are your tenant communication channels into one system of record?

answers.label: 1 — Email, personal phones, and texts scattered everywhere
2 — Most lives in email but key threads get lost
3 — Central inbox exists but staff still use side channels
4 — One inbox, all channels routed in, logged to property record
5 — Unified inbox with full history, auto-tagged, searchable by unit

questions.text: How automated is handling of routine tenant questions (rent balance, lease terms, amenity hours)?

answers.label: 1 — Every question gets a human reply from scratch
2 — Staff use copy-paste templates they maintain manually
3 — Portal FAQ deflects some but most still hits staff
4 — AI assistant answers routine questions, escalates edge cases
5 — Fully automated tenant assistant with CRM-grounded answers

low.headline: Your tenant inbox is the single biggest time sink
low.text: Scattered channels and slow response times are costing your team 10+ hours a week on triage alone. The first fix is not a chatbot — it is getting every channel into one logged inbox so you can actually see the volume.
steps: Pick one system of record (portal, shared inbox, or CRM) and route email, SMS, and voicemail transcripts into it this week
Write a 10-line response SLA and post it where staff can see it (ack within 1h, resolve within 24h)
Audit last month's inbox: tag the top 20 recurring questions — those become your first automation targets

mid.headline: Consolidated but still human-heavy
mid.text: You have structure, now you need leverage. Routine questions are eating your team's focus even when response times look acceptable. Add an AI assistant layer on top of your unified inbox to deflect the repeat 60%.
steps: Build an AI first-responder that pulls tenant ledger, lease, and unit data to answer the top 20 questions
Route anything it cannot answer to a human with the context pre-summarized
Measure deflection rate weekly — aim for 50%+ within 60 days

high.headline: Keep sharpening — here's the frontier
high.text: Response speed is a solved problem for you. The next gains come from proactive tenant communication that prevents tickets from being opened in the first place.
steps: Add proactive outreach: renewal nudges, rent reminders, and maintenance follow-ups triggered by events not calendars
Build sentiment scoring on inbound messages to flag churn risk to PMs before renewal window
Instrument 'questions we should not be getting' — feed them back into tenant onboarding and portal UX

questions.text: How standardized is your maintenance intake from first tenant report to assigned work order?

answers.label: 1 — Requests come in every channel, staff manually enter them
2 — Portal exists but tenants still call and email
3 — Most go through portal, staff triage and categorize by hand
4 — Intake form captures structured data, auto-routes by category
5 — Any-channel intake, AI categorizes urgency and vendor, creates work order

questions.text: How automated is vendor selection and dispatch once a work order is created?

answers.label: 1 — Staff calls or texts a vendor they remember is available
2 — Spreadsheet of vendors, manual pick based on trade
3 — Preferred vendor list by trade, rotated manually
4 — System auto-suggests vendor by trade, area, and past performance
5 — Auto-dispatch with vendor confirmation loop and tenant scheduling

questions.text: How proactive are status updates to tenants and owners through the lifecycle of a work order?

answers.label: 1 — Tenants and owners call us asking for updates
2 — Staff sends manual updates when they remember
3 — Updates at key milestones, sent by staff from templates
4 — Automated updates at status changes, staff handles exceptions
5 — Fully automated lifecycle updates with photos and ETA to both sides

questions.text: How automated is reconciling vendor invoices against the work order and owner statement?

answers.label: 1 — Paper or PDF invoices, bookkeeper matches manually
2 — Digital invoices but still manual matching and coding
3 — Invoices attached to work orders, manual posting to GL
4 — Invoice parsing auto-matches to work order and owner
5 — Straight-through: parse, match, approve by rule, post, notify owner

low.headline: Maintenance is where your team actually lives
low.text: Unstructured intake and manual dispatch are where the hours disappear. Every call that becomes a work order is 10-15 minutes of clerical work per ticket. Structured intake alone will return 5+ hours a week.
steps: Force every request through one intake form — even if staff fill it in on the tenant's behalf for a quarter
Build a vendor matrix: trade, area, response time, approved rate — make it the source of dispatch decisions
Template the three status updates that matter: received, scheduled, completed

mid.headline: Good intake, leaky middle
mid.text: Your pipeline captures work orders well but the vendor and invoice legs are still human-driven. That middle layer is where AI dispatch and invoice parsing pay back fastest — typically inside 90 days.
steps: Add AI categorization on intake that sets urgency, trade, and suggested vendor before a human sees it
Automate the status update layer completely — staff should never manually type 'vendor scheduled for Tuesday' again
Pilot invoice OCR + auto-match on your top 3 vendors first, then expand

high.headline: Keep sharpening — here's the frontier
high.text: You have a real maintenance operations engine. The next edge comes from predictive and portfolio-level intelligence — catching issues before tenants report them.
steps: Mine 12 months of work orders to predict seasonal spikes per property and pre-stage vendor capacity
Add owner-facing proactive reporting that flags properties trending above peer cost benchmarks
Build automated vendor scorecards and auto-retire underperformers without manager review

questions.text: How fast and consistent is your response to new rental inquiries across Zillow, Apartments.com, and direct?

answers.label: 1 — Leads get missed or answered a day later
2 — Leasing agents reply when they can, quality varies
3 — Shared inbox, same-day response, some templating
4 — Auto-response within minutes, human follow-up same hour
5 — AI handles qualification and booking in minutes, 24/7

questions.text: How automated is tour scheduling including self-showings and follow-up?

answers.label: 1 — All back-and-forth by phone and text to land a time
2 — Booking link exists but agents still coordinate manually often
3 — Online booking works but no reminders or no-show follow-up
4 — Booking + reminders + auto follow-up after tour
5 — Self-showing enabled where legal, full pre and post tour automation

questions.text: How automated is the application review and screening decision workflow?

answers.label: 1 — PDF applications emailed back and forth, manual credit and income checks
2 — Online application but staff reviews every field manually
3 — Screening service integrated, staff decides each file
4 — Rules-based auto-approve for clean files, escalate borderline
5 — Full decisioning workflow with documented rules and audit trail

low.headline: You are losing deals before your team sees the lead
low.text: Slow lead response and manual scheduling kill conversion. At your size, each unit that sits an extra week is measurable money, and leasing staff are spending their day on clerical work not conversion.
steps: Deploy instant lead auto-response tied to a booking link — this alone lifts tour rate 20-40%
Move application intake fully online and standardize the required documents
Write down your screening rules explicitly so they can be automated next

mid.headline: Pipeline works, decisions still bottleneck
mid.text: You capture leads cleanly, now it is the decision layer that slows you down. Rules-based screening and structured follow-up will compress days-to-lease without hiring.
steps: Codify your auto-approve, auto-decline, and escalate criteria as a written rule set
Automate the between-tour-and-application nudge sequence — most drop-off happens there
Build a weekly leasing dashboard (lead-to-tour, tour-to-app, app-to-signed) so the team sees their own funnel

high.headline: Keep sharpening — here's the frontier
high.text: Your leasing funnel is tight. The next gains are predictive pricing and portfolio-level vacancy intelligence.
steps: Add dynamic rent recommendations based on comps and days-on-market signal
Build vacancy forecasting 60 days out so marketing spend is front-loaded, not reactive
Deploy post-move-in sentiment capture at day 30 and day 90 to predict renewal

questions.text: How systematic is your delinquency workflow from day 1 late to resolution?

answers.label: 1 — Staff manually chases each delinquent tenant on their own schedule
2 — Standard reminder dates but sent manually
3 — Automated first notice, manual from there
4 — Full automated cadence, staff handles only non-responders
5 — Automated cadence + AI follow-up + structured payment plan offers

questions.text: How automated is monthly owner reporting and distribution?

answers.label: 1 — Bookkeeper builds owner reports mostly by hand
2 — Reports generated from software but need heavy manual cleanup
3 — Reports auto-generate, staff reviews and emails each owner
4 — Reports auto-generate and auto-distribute with narrative summary
5 — Owners get self-serve portal + AI-written performance summary

questions.text: How automated is handling of recurring owner questions (statements, draws, property performance)?

answers.label: 1 — PMs or bookkeeper answer every owner email individually
2 — Standard email templates but still human-sent
3 — Owner portal handles some, staff handles the rest
4 — AI assistant answers owner questions from live data
5 — Full owner AI assistant with ledger access and escalation rules

low.headline: Finance is quietly the most expensive department
low.text: Manual delinquency chasing and hand-built owner reports do not just cost hours, they cost consistency. Automating the cadence here usually frees a full FTE of bookkeeper time inside a quarter.
steps: Write the exact delinquency cadence (day 1, 3, 5, 10 notices) and automate the first three notices this month
Audit your owner report template — kill any field that requires manual lookup
Set a rule: any owner question asked more than 3x per month becomes a portal or automation target

mid.headline: Automated in parts, still human-gated
mid.text: The infrastructure is there, but humans are still the final step on too many workflows. Removing the manual review on reports and the manual answers on routine owner questions is the next leverage point.
steps: Move owner report distribution to fully automated with a standard AI-written narrative summary
Deploy an AI owner assistant that can answer statement and draw questions from live ledger data
Build structured payment plan offers into the delinquency cadence so staff stops negotiating one-off

high.headline: Keep sharpening — here's the frontier
high.text: Collections and reporting are running themselves. The frontier is portfolio-level financial intelligence — giving owners insight, not just reports.
steps: Build benchmark reporting: how each property performs vs peer properties in your portfolio
Add predictive delinquency scoring to flag at-risk tenants 30 days before they go late
Offer owners an AI financial analyst layer — 'what if I raised rents 3%, what if I replaced the roof' scenarios

questions.text: How documented and accessible are your standard operating procedures for daily tasks?

answers.label: 1 — SOPs live in people's heads, knowledge walks out the door
2 — Some docs exist in a shared drive, mostly outdated
3 — Core SOPs documented, not consistently followed
4 — Living SOP system, staff actually uses it
5 — SOPs are structured, searchable, and feed AI assistants staff use daily

questions.text: How fast and consistent is your onboarding of a new property manager or leasing agent?

answers.label: 1 — Shadow a teammate for a few weeks and hope they pick it up
2 — Checklist exists but ramp quality varies by trainer
3 — Structured 2-4 week plan, mostly shadowing-based
4 — Structured onboarding with measurable milestones and video training
5 — New hires productive in under 2 weeks via AI-assisted playbooks and Q&A

questions.text: How clean are handoffs between leasing, property management, maintenance, and accounting?

answers.label: 1 — Dropped balls and finger-pointing are a weekly occurrence
2 — Handoffs happen but rely on individual diligence
3 — Checklists exist, sometimes followed
4 — System-enforced handoffs with triggers and owners
5 — Workflow automation makes dropped handoffs structurally impossible

questions.text: How clear is your visibility as an owner into where time and money are actually going this week?

answers.label: 1 — I find out about problems when they blow up
2 — Weekly meetings are my main source of truth
3 — Dashboards exist but I have to go hunt them down
4 — Weekly automated digest of the key operational metrics hits my inbox
5 — Real-time view with AI-written weekly narrative of what changed and why

low.headline: Your operation is running on tribal knowledge
low.text: Undocumented SOPs and loose handoffs are the hidden reason every new hire takes 3 months to ramp and every vacation creates a crisis. Documentation is not a vanity project at your size — it is the single biggest unlock.
steps: Pick the 10 workflows your team runs most often and document them as screen-recorded walkthroughs this month
Define handoff triggers in writing: leasing to PM, PM to maintenance, maintenance to accounting
Set up a weekly owner digest — even a manual one — so you stop finding out about problems on Fridays

mid.headline: Documented but not leveraged
mid.text: You have the raw material, now you need to turn it into leverage. SOPs that sit in a folder do nothing; SOPs that feed an AI assistant your team actually uses change your ramp time and your handoff quality overnight.
steps: Move your SOPs into a structured, searchable format and make them the source for an internal AI assistant
Enforce handoffs in your software, not in meetings — triggers, required fields, owners
Replace the weekly ops meeting with an automated digest plus a 20-minute exceptions-only review

high.headline: Keep sharpening — here's the frontier
high.text: Your team runs on systems, not heroes. The next edge is turning your operational data into forward-looking intelligence — and making the org truly hire-and-plug-in.
steps: Build a '2-week productive' new hire path using AI-assisted playbooks and shadowing transcripts
Instrument every handoff with cycle-time metrics and publish them — what gets measured gets faster
Add predictive staffing: model which weeks your team will be underwater 30 days out

ctas.headline: Want a working call on your property management ops score?
ctas.description: Book a 30-minute working call where I'll walk you through your exact answers, stack them against property management firms your size, and pinpoint the ONE fix I'd prioritize first. No pitch — if we both see signal, we keep going.

ctas.headline: Under 20 employees? You're early for a custom audit.
ctas.description: Automation ROI at your scale is real but payback is longer and the obvious fixes are usually process before tooling. Follow me on LinkedIn where I post tactical property management automation breakdowns 3x a week. When you cross 20 employees, come back and retake — the CTA will change.

ctas.headline: You're running tight — here's what property management firms your size miss next.
ctas.description: At your score, the low-hanging fruit is already picked. The next gains come from predictive maintenance and vacancy modeling, AI-written owner narratives that actually drive retention, and structural handoff instrumentation that makes dropped balls impossible. I'm writing teardowns on each — follow along.

ctas.headline: Get the weekly property management ops teardown
ctas.description: One tactical case study plus one n8n workflow breakdown per week. How mid-market property management firms run lean, scale without scaling payroll, and hit the 90-Day Payback Rule on every automation. No fluff, no pitch.
